items.title: Vibe CLI /model command flow
items.text:
Here's an explanation of what happens when you invoke the /model command in the Vibe CLI interface:

sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant VibeCLI
    participant ModelPickerApp
    participant VibeConfig
    participant AgentLoop

    User->>VibeCLI: /model a_model
    VibeCLI->>VibeCLI: Check if model picker is already active
    alt Model picker not active
        VibeCLI->>ModelPickerApp: Create ModelPickerApp
        ModelPickerApp->>VibeConfig: Get available models
        VibeConfig-->>ModelPickerApp: Return model aliases
        ModelPickerApp->>ModelPickerApp: Initialize with model aliases
        VibeCLI->>VibeCLI: Switch to model picker UI
        VibeCLI->>VibeCLI: Hide chat input container
        VibeCLI->>VibeCLI: Update current bottom app state
        VibeCLI->>ModelPickerApp: Mount in bottom container
        VibeCLI->>ModelPickerApp: Focus the widget
    else Model picker already active
        VibeCLI->>VibeCLI: Do nothing
    end

    User->>ModelPickerApp: Select a_model
    ModelPickerApp->>VibeCLI: on_model_picker_app_model_selected event
    VibeCLI->>VibeConfig: Save updates with new active model
    VibeCLI->>VibeCLI: Reload configuration
    VibeCLI->>AgentLoop: Reload with initial messages
    VibeCLI->>VibeCLI: Resolve plan
    VibeCLI->>VibeCLI: Sync narrator manager
    VibeCLI->>VibeCLI: Update banner state
    VibeCLI->>VibeCLI: Show confirmation message
    VibeCLI->>VibeCLI: Switch back to input app
    VibeCLI->>VibeCLI: Show model switched message

items.title: Workflow Explanation:
items.text:
1. User Invokes Command:
  2. The user types /model a_model in the Vibe CLI interface

  3. This triggers the _switch_to_model_picker_app method

  4. Model Picker Initialization:

  5. The CLI checks if the model picker is already active
  6. If not, it creates a new ModelPickerApp instance
  7. The model picker gets the list of available models from the configuration

  8. The model picker initializes with the available models and current selection

  9. UI Switching:

  10. The chat input container is hidden
  11. The current bottom app state is updated to ModelPicker
  12. The model picker is mounted in the bottom container

  13. The model picker widget is focused

  14. Model Selection:

  15. The user selects a model from the picker

  16. This triggers the on_model_picker_app_model_selected event handler

  17. Configuration Update:

  18. The new model is saved in the configuration
  19. The configuration is reloaded
  20. The agent loop is reloaded with initial messages
  21. The plan is resolved
  22. The narrator manager is synced

  23. The banner state is updated

  24. UI Update:

  25. A confirmation message is shown
  26. The UI switches back to the input app
  27. A message confirming the model switch is displayed

This workflow shows the complete process from when you type the /model command until the UI updates to reflect the new model selection. The actual model switching happens through the configuration system, which then triggers the necessary UI updates and agent loop reloads.

items.title: Install Mistral Vibe locally from git repo using uv
Install Mistral Vibe locally from git repo
items.text:
After cloning the mistral-vibe repository, you can run the tests using the following steps:

1) Navigate to the repository directory:

cd mistral-vibe

2) Install the package in development mode (this will install all dependencies):

uv pip install -e .

3) Run the tests:

uv run pytest

4) For more detailed output, you can add the -v flag:

uv run pytest -v

5) To run specific tests, you can specify the test file or directory:

uv run pytest tests/test_core.py

6) To run tests with coverage, you can use the following command:

uv run pytest --cov=vibe

These commands will run the test suite for mistral-vibe using pytest. The -e flag in the installation command installs the package in development mode, which is necessary for running the tests.

items.title: Quickly running pytest
items.text:
After cloning the mistral-vibe repo (this assumes that uv is installed)

1) Navigate to the repository directory:

cd mistral-vibe

2) Trigger tests: 

uv run pytest -v

This will automatically create a virtual environment and run the tests.

items.title: Installing Mistral Vibe locally (multiple instances/versions of it)
items.text:
Sometimes, it's interesting to have multiple instances of Mistral Vibe installed. This is easily achieved using Python virtualenv e.g. through uv.

The following is especially useful when one wants to use a fresh config.toml file. More specifically, an already installed instance of vibe could be located at:

~/.local/bin/vibe

but actually pointing to:

~/.local/share/uv/tools/mistral-vibe/bin/vibe

Now, installing another instance of vibe in another virtualenv would still pick up the config.toml file which is by default located:

~/.vibe/config.toml

Depending on the use case, this can be useful and especially for re-using the locally stored API keys.

However, if one wants to have a fresh config.toml file, we need an additional step. For demonstration purposes, we use the /tmp/ directory:

1) Create a directory:

mkdir /tmp/vibe-test

2) Set and expose the VIBE_HOME variable to this directory:

export VIBE_HOME=/tmp/vibe-test

3) Create and activate your virtualenv and install Mistral Vibe:

uv venv 
Using CPython 3.14.2
Creating virtual environment at: .venv

source .venv/bin/activate
uv pip install git+https://github.com/ph4ge/mistral-vibe mistral-vibe # using my fork
vibe will be installed: 
(.venv)/tmp/vibe-test$ which vibe
/tmp/vibe-test/.venv/bin/vibe

4) Run Mistral Vibe:

vibe
This will create a fresh config.toml file in /tmp/vibe-test
